straight_beat.name: Hal Cifuentes
straight_beat.column:
The Angels lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to rip your hair out. You know the script: 10 runs scored, a bullpen that coughed up two in the eighth, and a team that’s somehow still managing to flounder. Here’s the box score talking.  

The offense was lively, that’s for sure. Ten runs is a lot of runs, and you’d think that’d be enough to beat a team that’s been struggling to stay in games. But here’s the thing about baseball: you don’t win by scoring runs. You win by scoring runs *and* not letting the other team do the same. The Yankees, for all their flaws, found a way to stay ahead. And the Angels? They kept finding ways to let the other team

optimist.name: Sunny Escalante
optimist.column: The Angels lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. Let’s talk about the way Rengifo flipped that double play in the third. That’s October magic hiding in April. Sure, it was a 10-11 loss, but there’s a rhythm to this team now—a pulse beneath the surface that’s hard to ignore. The way Rengifo turned a potential inning

pessimist.name: Murray Doblinski
pessimist.column:
The Angels lost again. Not because they’re bad, but because they’re *still* the Angels. You know, the ones who’ve been waiting for Nolan Ryan to come back and pitch a no-hitter in the 2020s. The ones who’ve been handed a roster full of “potential” and “tools” and “prospects” since Arte Moreno started treating the franchise like a trust fund. Last night’s 10-11 defeat to the Yankees wasn’t a catastrophe—it was a reminder of why we’re all still here, sipping lukewarm coffee and watching the standings tick backward.  

The offense had its moments. Shohei Ohtani, for all his brilliance, still can’t hit a slider to save his life. And when you’ve got a guy who can hit 40 home runs but can’t clear the infield, you’re not exactly building a team that *wins* games. The Yankees’ offense didn’t just outslug
